query: What Con Edison commercial customer service class pays the highest demand charge in Manhattan based on OpenEI tariffs data?
final_answer:
Assuming “Manhattan” means Con Edison’s NYC service territory, which Con Edison maps to NYISO **Zone J = N.Y.C.** (and the OpenEI tariff names also label the relevant Manhattan rates as **[NYC]**) (Con Edison Capacity and Energy Reconciliation Guidelines PDF: https://www.coned.com/-/media/files/coned/documents/business-partners/escos-news/2025/14-capacity-energy-guidelines.pdf; OpenEI IURDB Con Edison tariff records via `get_utility_tariffs`, utility ID 4226):

**The Con Edison commercial service class with the highest posted demand charge in Manhattan is `SC-8 - Multiple Dwellings Redistribution Low Tension Service - Zone J [NYC]`.**  
In the OpenEI tariff record, its highest demand tier is **$53.85/kW-month plus a $14.69/kW adjustment = $68.54/kW-month** for the `Delivery with Standard Offer` version. (OpenEI tariff record `67b6667e54c99cee7203bf9b` in the `get_utility_tariffs` output.)

For comparison, the next-highest NYC/Zone J commercial demand charge I found was `SC-12 - Multiple Dwellings Space Heating Low Tension Service - Zone J [NYC]` at **$60.19/kW-month plus $6.58/kW = $66.77/kW-month**. (OpenEI tariff record `67b8d83811f07bd55506da3c` / `67b8d5056294012a6406fbfc` in the `get_utility_tariffs` output.)

So the answer is: **SC-8 Multiple Dwellings Redistribution Low Tension Service (Zone J/NYC)**.

(CECONY) in the New York Independent System Operator (NYISO) Administered Markets.\n\nCECONY is the Transmission Owner (TO) and Metering Authority for electric load in three NYISO subzones.\n\nZone ID Zone Name Subzone PTID Subzone Name\n\n| | H | MILLWD | | 80482 | | CONED MILLWOOD | | |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| | I | DUNWOD | | 55532 | | CONED DUNWOODIE | | |\n| | J | N.Y.C. | | 55523 | | CONED NYCITY | | |\n\nCECONY is responsible to provide Capacity and Energy Reconciliation services to Energy Supply Companies operating in these market subzones. An Energy Supply Company (ESCO) is a non-utility business deemed eligible by the New York State Public Service Commission (PSC) and approved by CECONY to sell electricity to end users within the CECONY Transmission District (TD).\n\nThis guide also uses the term Load Serving Entity (LSE) to describe an ESCO. The NYISO defines a LSE in their Market Services Tariff as a company that supplies Energy, Capacity and/or Ancillary Services to retail customers located within the New York Control Area (NYCA). This definition applies to both an ESCOs and to CECONY as the utility providing load to customers that have not elected to purchase energy from an ESCO.\n\nCECONY performs Capacity Load Forecast and Load Shift reporting to the NYISO for all LSEs in the TD. In this guide, the term Capacity refers to the Load Forecast Tag CECONY calculates for each account. The NYISO uses the account Load Forecast Tags to calculate Minimum Unforced Capacity Requirements for Load Serving Entities.\n\nCECONY also submits Transmission Owner Load (TOL) reports to the NYISO. The TOL assigns a proportional share of the wholesale subzone load (MLOAD) to each LSE. This manual refers to TOL reporting as Energy Reconciliation.\n\nAs of May 1, 2016, the Transmission Owner Data Reporting System (TODRS) has replaced the legacy CAP (Capacity) and RECON (Reconciliation) computer applications that retrieve customer billing data to perform routine calculations and data reporting. A CECONY analyst uses the TODRS to fulfill CECONY’s responsibilities to the NYISO and LSEs.\n\nExplanation of Load Shapes\n\nThe TODRS utilizes standardized load shapes to develop the hourly usage for accounts without interval data recorders. This system also uses load shapes when the recorder data for an account is unavailable or unreliable.\n\nThe current file of load shapes is available for download on the CECONY Retail Access Information System (RAIS) website.\n\nDefinition of Terms\n\nLoad Shape is a series of 24 time-ordered values representing the hourly demand of a customer or group of customers. CECONY Rate Engineering develops load shapes from sample data as part of the company’s Load Research Program.\n\nLoad shape selection depends on the following criteria (Load Shape Selection Criteria):\n\n| • | Day type | | |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| • | Service Class | | |\n| • | Stratum | | |\n| • | Temperature Reference | | |\n\nDay Type is an indicator of the calendar specific characteristics of a daily load shape. The TODRS assigns a representative load shape based upon the day of the week and holiday criteria.\n\nThe Day Type codes are:\n\n| MON | | - Monday | |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| TUE | | - Tuesday | |\n| WED | | - Wednesday | |\n| THU | | - Thursday | |\n| FRI | | - Friday | |\n| SAT | | - Saturday | |\n| SUN | | - Sunday | |\n| HOL | | - Weekday holidays where load does not follow the normal weekday shape | |\n\nHoliday Day Type Schedule\n\nNew Year’s Day Memorial Day\n\nJuneteenth \nIndependence Day \nLabor Day \nThanksgiving Day \nChristmas Eve \nChristmas Day \nDay After Christmas \nNew Year’s Eve \n\nService Class represents a group of customer types with similar load characteristics. The Con Edison tariff establishes the Service Classes definitions\n\nStratum Category represents a subgroup within a service class. It is a measure of the size of a customer as defined by a particular billing quantity. CECONY refers to this billing quantity as the Stratum Billing Variable. The table below relates a Stratum Billing Variable to the applicable Service Class.\n\n| Service Class | Service Class Description | Stratum Billing Variable |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| SC 1 | Residential (excluding Religious and Water Heating) | Annual kWh |\n| SC 1 WH | Residential & Religious Water Heating | Annual kWh |\n| SC 2 | General Small Commercial | June-September kWh |\n| SC 5 | Electric Traction Systems | Annual kWh |\n| SC 6 | Private Street Lighting | Month of Year |\n| SC 7 | Residential Space Heating & Water Heating | Annual kWh |\n| SC 8 | Multiple Dwelling Redistribution | Average of June-September kW |\n| SC 9 | General Large Commercial (excluding Space Heating) | Average of June-September kW |\n| SC 12 | Multiple Dwelling Space Heating | November-February kWh |\n\n| Service Class Service Class Description | Stratum Billing Variable |\n| --- | --- |\n| | Annual kWh |\n| SC 1 WH Residential & Religious Water Heating | Annual kWh |\n| SC 2 General Small Commercial | June-September kWh |\n| SC 5 Electric Traction Systems | Annual kWh |\n| SC 6 Private Street Lighting | Month of Year |\n| SC 7 Residential Space Heating & Water Heating | Annual kWh |\n| SC 8 Multiple Dwelling Redistribution | Average of June-September kW |\n| General Large Commercial (excluding Space SC 9 | Average of June-September kW |\n| SC 12 Multiple Dwelling Space Heating | November-February kWh |\n\nThe following sub-classes have separate load shapes developed from their corresponding load research data.\n\nService Class Service Class Description Stratum Billing Variable\n\nSC 51 Religious June-September kWh\n\nThe Stratum Billing Variable in-use is derived from the most recent monthly billing information available.\n\nTemperature Variable (TV) represents the temperature-based value associated with a load shape. Once the value is computed for a particular day, a load shape appropriate for the temperature within a given range can be selected. The temperature for the period May 1 st through October 31 st is based on a three-day weighted average of daily 24-hour dry/wet bulb temperatures as measured in Central Park and La Guardia. For the period November 1 st through April 30 th it is based on a three-day weighted average of daily 24-hour dry bulb temperatures as measured in Central Park and LaGuardia. Executive Summary\n\nOn December 18, 2025, the Commission directed Con Edison to develop a “NYC Reliability Contingency \nPlan” and to begin by filing a report with the Commission identifying the specific reliability needs that \nCon Edison sees arising in its service territory, the dates of those needs, and the underlying assumptions \nand methodologies used in determining those needs.1 The Commission further directed Con Edison to \nupdate its analysis every six months. \nAs of January 2026, Con Edison projects a reliability need in the New York City 345/138 kV Transmission \nLoad Area (TLA)2 of 125 MW in 2032, reaching 750 MW by 2036. The details behind this assessment are \nexplained in this report as required by the Commission. \nThe January 2026 reassessment 3 refines and updates the December 2025 preliminary assessment Con \nEdison presented at the NYISO. 4 In December 2025, Con Edison identified reliability needs starting at \n250 MW in 2030 and increasing to 1,325 MW in 2035, based on the Company’s 2024 load projection. \nThe January 2026 reassessment incorporates 2025 load projections, which use the 2025 Distribution \nLoad Area (DLA) peak demand forecast using a more granular 24-hour load modeling methodology \nwhich reduced the reliability need, offset in part by a 175 MW reduction in capacity sales from PJM, \nwhich increased the need. \nFurther, Con Edison projects capacity shortfalls in the overall New York City 345/138 kV TLA to continue \nto grow beyond the planning period covered by this report, reaching 925 MW by 2037 and 2,350 MW by \n2045. These increasing capacity shortfalls result from a large number of factors, some of which offset \neach other. On the customer and demand side, shortfalls are driven principally by continued economic \ngrowth in New York City as well as building and transportation electrification aided by state and local \nprograms that advance the ambitious goals established by the Climate Leadership and Community \nProtection Act (CLCPA), and offset partially by the adoption of distribution battery energy storage \nsystems (BESS) and continued increases in energy efficiency. On the supply side, the shortfalls are driven \nprimarily by generation retirements and state law limiting electric generation emissions, offset in part by \ngradual ramping up of intermittent clean and renewable development. steps.content:
Assuming “Manhattan” means Con Edison’s NYC service territory, which Con Edison maps to NYISO **Zone J = N.Y.C.** (and the OpenEI tariff names also label the relevant Manhattan rates as **[NYC]**) (Con Edison Capacity and Energy Reconciliation Guidelines PDF: https://www.coned.com/-/media/files/coned/documents/business-partners/escos-news/2025/14-capacity-energy-guidelines.pdf; OpenEI IURDB Con Edison tariff records via `get_utility_tariffs`, utility ID 4226):

**The Con Edison commercial service class with the highest posted demand charge in Manhattan is `SC-8 - Multiple Dwellings Redistribution Low Tension Service - Zone J [NYC]`.**  
In the OpenEI tariff record, its highest demand tier is **$53.85/kW-month plus a $14.69/kW adjustment = $68.54/kW-month** for the `Delivery with Standard Offer` version. (OpenEI tariff record `67b6667e54c99cee7203bf9b` in the `get_utility_tariffs` output.)

For comparison, the next-highest NYC/Zone J commercial demand charge I found was `SC-12 - Multiple Dwellings Space Heating Low Tension Service - Zone J [NYC]` at **$60.19/kW-month plus $6.58/kW = $66.77/kW-month**. (OpenEI tariff record `67b8d83811f07bd55506da3c` / `67b8d5056294012a6406fbfc` in the `get_utility_tariffs` output.)

So the answer is: **SC-8 Multiple Dwellings Redistribution Low Tension Service (Zone J/NYC)**.
